diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Deep ache from ciliary spasm is characteristic of intraocular inflammation, not surface conjunctival disease.
Conjunctivitis causes surface grittiness/itch, not a deep intraocular ache.
Angle-closure also produces severe eye pain — cannot be excluded on pain character alone.
Consensual photophobia (pain on light in either eye) is a hallmark of anterior uveitis.
Corneal involvement also produces marked photophobia.

symptoms.symptom_name: Unilateral red eye without purulent or watery discharge

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Uveitic redness is non-discharging; absence of discharge helps distinguish from infective conjunctivitis.
Conjunctivitis typically produces watery or purulent discharge with stickiness — absent here.

symptoms.symptom_name: Absence of nausea, vomiting, severe headache or haloes

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: These systemic/visual features often accompany angle-closure; their absence lowers but does not exclude the probability without measured intraocular pressure.

symptoms.symptom_name: No trauma, chemical exposure or contact-lens wear

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Contact-lens wear and trauma are major keratitis risk factors; their absence lowers pre-test probability but a dendritic ulcer still cannot be excluded remotely.
Inflammatory back stiffness raises suspicion of HLA-B27 spondyloarthropathy, the classic systemic association of recurrent acute anterior uveitis.
Deep circumcorneal injection indicates intraocular inflammation — a slit-lamp/exam sign not obtainable via telehealth.

symptoms.symptom_name: Sluggish, miotic pupil in the affected eye

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Iris sphincter spasm produces a small, sluggish pupil in uveitis — and a fixed mid-dilated pupil would instead point to angle-closure. An examination finding, sealed.
Angle-closure classically gives a fixed mid-dilated pupil, not a miotic one — but this distinction requires examination.
Normal intraocular pressure is what excludes acute angle-closure — and it can only be assessed in person, which is precisely why remote exclusion is unsafe.

symptoms.symptom_name: Anterior-chamber cells and flare on slit-lamp

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Cells and flare in the anterior chamber are the defining slit-lamp finding of anterior uveitis — never available in telehealth.

symptoms.symptom_name: Fluorescein staining for corneal epithelial defect / dendritic ulcer

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Fluorescein staining reveals a dendritic ulcer; excluding herpetic keratitis is mandatory before any topical steroid — an in-person investigation.

symptom_clusters.cluster_name: Painful photophobic non-discharging unilateral red eye
symptom_clusters.clinical_significance: Together these move the picture away from conjunctivitis and toward an intraocular/serious cause requiring urgent in-person eye assessment.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Slit-lamp biomicroscopy of the anterior segment
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Defines anterior-chamber cells/flare (uveitis) and inspects the cornea — the core in-person examination this telehealth encounter cannot perform.
Excludes acute angle-closure glaucoma — the sight-threatening mimic that cannot be excluded remotely.
Excludes a dendritic (herpetic) ulcer before any topical corticosteroid is initiated.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: HLA-B27 typing and rheumatology review for back symptoms
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Downstream systemic work-up for recurrent anterior uveitis with inflammatory back stiffness — not needed acutely but should be arranged.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Empirical topical antibiotic prescription without examination
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: This is not conjunctivitis; empirical antibiotics do not treat uveitis and delay appropriate specialist assessment.
